input_text: Insulin is required for all patients with type 1 diabetes mellitus because they do not produce adequate insulin (due to destruction of the pancreatic beta-cells) and will develop ketoacidosis without it.

_entries.subject: type 1 diabetes mellitus
_entries.subject_type_definition: A chronic metabolic disorder characterized by the body's inability to produce insulin due to the autoimmune destruction of pancreatic beta-cells.
_entries.relation_definition: The subject disease or condition may be alleviated or managed through the use of the specified drug, procedure, or intervention.
_entries.object_type_definition: A pharmacological agent prescribed to prevent, treat, or manage a disease or clinical condition.

input_text: Insulin is also used in the management of many patients with type 2 diabetes.

input_text: To achieve this, different preparations of insulin and methods of administration can be used.
Except for use of regular insulin, which is given IV in hospitalized patients, insulin is almost always administered subcutaneously.

input_text: An inhaled insulin preparation is available for patients who prefer not to inject themselves.
oie_raw: patients who prefer not to inject themselves

input_text: It has a slightly more rapid onset of action compared to subcutaneously injected rapid acting insulin.
oie_raw: slightly more rapid onset of action

input_text: Multiple daily subcutaneous injections administered by the patient, with 2 preparations of insulin used depending on the anticipated glycemic control needs.

input_text: An insulin pump that delivers a rapid- or short-acting insulin and administers a basal rate of insulin and additional boluses with meals or for correcting a high blood glucose level.
Both strategies require close attention to diet and exercise as well as to insulin timing and dose.
